study_notes.title: The Axial Skeleton: Protection and Support
study_notes.introduction: The axial skeleton forms the vertical, central axis of the human body. It is primarily designed to protect vital internal organs and support the weight of the head and trunk.

major_parts: Cranium: The rigid bony 'brain box' that shields the delicate brain.
Facial Bones: 14 bones that form the orbits (eye sockets), nasal cavity, and jaw.
Mandible: The only movable bone in the skull, used for chewing and speech.
Sutures: Immovable, jagged joints that lock skull bones together for maximum protection.
the_human_skull.infant_skull_facts: Newborns have soft spots called 'fontanelles'. These allow the skull bones to overlap slightly during birth and provide space for rapid brain growth.

the_vertebral_column_spine.definition: A flexible column made of 33 small bones in infants, which fuse to 26 bones in adults.
primary_functions: Protects the spinal cord (the body's main nerve highway).
Supports the head and upright posture of the trunk.
Provides an anchor point for the rib cage.

five_regions_of_the_spine.Cervical: 7 bones (Neck). Contains the Atlas (supports skull) and Axis (allows head rotation).
five_regions_of_the_spine.Thoracic: 12 bones (Chest). Each attaches to a pair of ribs.
five_regions_of_the_spine.Lumbar: 5 bones (Lower back). The largest and thickest, supporting the most weight.
five_regions_of_the_spine.Sacral: 5 fused vertebrae (Sacrum). Connects the spine to the pelvic girdle.
five_regions_of_the_spine.Coccygeal: 4 fused vertebrae (Coccyx/Tailbone). The vestigial base of the spine.

rib_cage.sternum: The central, flat 'breastplate' that anchors the ribs at the front.

rib_classification.True_Ribs: Pairs 1-7. Attach directly to the sternum via their own costal cartilage.
rib_classification.False_Ribs: Pairs 8-10. Attach indirectly to the sternum through the cartilage of the 7th rib.
rib_classification.Floating_Ribs: Pairs 11-12. No front attachment; they protect the lower back and kidneys.

breathing_mechanics: Inhalation: Ribs move UP and OUT, increasing chest volume, lowering pressure, drawing air in.
Exhalation: Ribs move DOWN and IN, decreasing chest volume, forcing air out.

key_biomechanical_concepts: Intervertebral Discs: Pads of shock-absorbing cartilage between vertebrae.
S-Shaped Curvature: The spine is not a straight stick; it is curved to act as a spring, balancing weight and absorbing shocks during walking/running.
Neural Arch: The bony hole in each vertebra that creates the protective tunnel for the spinal cord.
study_tips_for_ple: The Rib Cage protects the Heart and Lungs, while the Skull protects the Brain.
Costal cartilage is flexible, which is the ONLY reason we can breathe. Solid bone would make breathing impossible!
If you stand hunched (bad posture), you are compressing your own lungs and putting stress on your thoracic spine.
